Token count must be at least 1: Token count must be at least 1
Ballot tokens allow voters to access the: Ballot tokens allow voters to access their unique voting page. Share these links individually or export all as CSV.
Important: Each token should be shared w: Important: Each token should be shared with exactly one person. Tokens are unique to each voter and should not be reused or shared publicly.
No ballot tokens have been created yet.
addCandidatesInstructions:
Add your candidates

Enter candidate names below.
- Click Add Candidate to add more.
- Click Remove to delete a candidate.
- You can rename candidates at any time.
STV-MD: Winners are identified without r: STV-MD: Winners are identified without ranking. STV-MD Copeland: Winners are ranked by position using pairwise comparison.
Each cell shows how many voters preferre: Each cell shows how many voters preferred the row candidate over the column candidate
Candidates are ordered by their final ra: Candidates are ordered by their final ranking (Copeland method). Green cells indicate wins, yellow indicates ties.
Admin Guide meta description: Complete guide for election administrators. Learn how to create elections, generate ballot tokens, distribute them to voters, and publish results.
Set the title, description, candidates,: Set the title, description, candidates, number of seats, and start/end dates.
When you create an election, you'll rece: When you create an election, you'll receive a unique admin page URL. Save this link and share it only with other trusted administrators.
On the admin page, generate ballot token: On the admin page, generate ballot tokens for each voter. Each token is a unique secret link that grants access to one ballot.
Generate as many tokens as you have elig: Generate as many tokens as you have eligible voters. You can create tokens in batches.
Send each token link individually to the: Send each token link individually to the respective voter. The voter should redeem the token privately - only they will have access to their ballot.
Important: tokens can only be redeemed w: Important: tokens can only be redeemed while the election is open. The system ensures ballot anonymity: administrators cannot see which ballot corresponds to which token.
Track voting progress on the admin page. You can see how many tokens have been redeemed and how many ballots have been cast.
You cannot see how individual voters vot: You cannot see how individual voters voted. All ballots are anonymous once cast.
After the voting period ends, results ar: After the voting period ends, results are calculated automatically and published on the public election page.
The system shows elected candidates, Cop: The system shows elected candidates, Copeland ranking, detailed counting rounds, and the pairwise comparison matrix.
This guide is for election administrator: This guide is for election administrators - members of an electoral commission or individuals delegated by the commission to manage an election.
This is where election results are publi: This is where election results are published and voters can view election details. Safe to share publicly.
This page is created when you set up a n: This page is created when you set up a new election and provides full control: generate tokens, edit details, and manage voting. Share only with trusted administrators.
When creating an election, you can choos: When creating an election, you can choose the election type:
Winners are ranked by position (1st, 2nd: Winners are ranked by position (1st, 2nd, 3rd place, etc.). Use this when the order matters - for example, when electing a president, vice-president, and secretary, or when determining seniority in a committee.
Technical note: After STV determines the: Technical note: After STV determines the winners, they are ranked using Copeland's pairwise comparison method based on voter preferences.
Winners are identified without ranking t: Winners are identified without ranking them. Use this when all elected candidates have equal status - for example, when filling multiple board member positions where all members have the same authority.
This ballot id does not exist: This ballot id does not exist
Ballot meta description: Cast your ballot in this STV election. Rank candidates by preference to participate in democratic decision-making.
Private Ballot: Do not share this page U: Private Ballot: Do not share this page URL with anyone. This link is unique to your vote and should be kept confidential.
Select your preferred rank for each cand: Select your preferred rank for each candidate. 1 is most preferred.
Number of seats must be between 1 and th: Number of seats must be between 1 and the number of candidates
Start and end times are required: Start and end times are required
End time must be after start time: End time must be after start time
Election Admin meta description: Administrative interface for managing elections. Create elections, generate ballot tokens, and monitor voting progress.
This page allows full control over the e: This page allows full control over the election. Only share this URL with people you trust to administer this election.
Title, description, and candidates are l: Title, description, and candidates are locked because voting tokens have been redeemed.
Results will be available after voting i: Results will be available after voting is complete
Meta description: Secure, transparent, and auditable STV election platform. Run proportional representation elections with Meek's method, manage ballot tokens, and provide full result transparency.
Meta description short: Secure, transparent, and auditable STV election platform. Run proportional representation elections with Meek's method.
heroSubtitle: Fair, transparent, and proportional ranked-choice voting
createElectionDesc: Set up your own STV election. Choose from three election types — standard STV, Copeland-ordered, or grouped (parity) — define candidates and voting periods, and distribute secure ballot tokens. Perfect for clubs, organizations, and communities.
experimentDesc: Try the interactive simulator to understand how STV works. Create your own elections, add candidates, and see results in real-time.
whatIsSTVPara1: Single Transferable Vote (STV) is a proportional voting system that ensures fair representation. Voters rank candidates in order of preference, and the algorithm ensures that votes are not wasted — if your top choice can't win or has already won, your vote transfers to your next preference.
whatIsSTVPara2: This makes STV ideal for electing multiple winners in a single contest, giving minority groups a fair chance while respecting majority preferences. It's used in real-world elections around the globe, from local councils to national legislatures.
technicalDetailsPara1: This website implements STV using Meek's method with the Droop quota, a precise and widely recognized algorithm for proportional ranked-choice elections. The underlying counting engine is open source, developed by Guillaume Endignoux, an engineer at Google.
technicalDetailsPara2: To provide a podium-style ranking of elected candidates, we augment the STV results using Copeland's method — a well-known approach based on pairwise comparison of preferences across all ballots. This means candidates are first selected by STV for fairness, then ordered according to voter preference strength.
technicalDetailsFooter: All election data, ballots, and detailed logs are published after voting closes, ensuring complete transparency and auditability.
readyToStartDesc: Browse active elections to cast your vote, or try the simulator to explore how STV works with your own scenarios.
Simulate meta description: Simulate STV elections with custom candidates and ballots. Test different scenarios and see detailed counting rounds and results.
editBallotsInstructions:
How to edit ballots:
- Enter the number of voters represented by each ballot.
- Rank the candidates (1 = first choice, 2 = second, ...); leave blank for "no preference".
- Candidates ranked equally are counted equally.
- Use Add Ballot group for new voting groups.
- Remove ballots you don't need.
loadSaveYamlInstructions:
Load/Save election YAML
Paste valid YAML to update the election setup.
Copy this field to export the current configuration.
Changes will apply automatically if the YAML is valid.
STV Explainer meta description: Learn how Single Transferable Vote (STV) works. Understand proportional representation, quota calculations, and the Meek method for fair elections.
stvMd.keyFeatureMinWaste: Minimizes the number of wasted votes
stvMd.keyFeatureComplex: At the expense of a more complicated counting method
stvMd.keyFeatureMinTactical: Reduces tactical voting: voters can honestly rank all preferences without fear of wasting their vote
stvMd.inputSeats: A number of seats to elect (`n`)
stvMd.inputVotes: A set of valid votes (total `t`), where each assigns a rank to any number of candidates.
stvMd.transferDefeated: When the highest preference is defeated, the vote is transferred to the next preference
stvMd.transferElected: When the highest preference is elected, the proportion of the vote not used to elect them is transferred to the next preference
stvMd.noWasteCaseMajority: a single candidate has a very high preference by the majority
stvMd.noWasteCaseMinority: a candidate has a very high preference by a minority
stvMd.refUNSW: University of New South Wales video on STV
stvMd.refElectoralCommission: Explanation of STV by Electoral Commission UK
stvMd.intro1: The STV-MD (Single Transferable Vote with Meek counting and Droop quota) method is a unimonial proportional representation voting system for multi-winner elections, combining Meek's iterative transfer algorithm with the Droop quota to fairly allocate seats and accurately reflect the voters' ranked preferences.
stvMd.intro2: It is used to elect N seats out of M candidates.
stvMd.inputsIntro: The method requires votes where voters rank candidates by preference. So, it needs:
stvMd.liveExampleDescription: Below is a concrete election with 5 candidates competing for 3 seats. The simulation runs automatically to show you exactly how STV works in practice.
stvMd.inputsExampleDescription: In this example, the election has:
ballotExplanation: Each row shows a group of voters who ranked candidates identically. The arrow (→) means 'preferred over'.
stvMd.iterationsIntro2: At the start, each vote counts 1, and this value is assigned to the highest preference(s). On each iteration, candidates are either classified as defeated (lowest tally of highest preference among votes) or elected (above the threshold to be elected). When defeated or elected, the vote is transferred to the next candidate as follows:
stvMd.noWasteIntro: This ensures that votes on both cases where
stvMd.noWasteConclusion: are not wasted because, in both cases, the "remaining" of the vote is transferred to the vote's next preference.
stepByStepIntro: Each round of counting is shown below. The quota line (amber) marks the votes needed for election. The blue bar for each candidate shows their vote count — darker blue for votes above the quota (surplus).
allVotesFullValue: All votes start at full value (1.0). The first preferences of each ballot are counted.
electExplanation: {{candidate}} has received enough votes to exceed the Droop quota. They are elected, and their surplus votes (votes above the quota) will be transferred to remaining candidates based on voters' next preferences.
defeatExplanation: {{candidate}} has the fewest votes ({{reason}}). They are defeated, and their votes are transferred to the next active preference on each ballot.
iterateExplanation: Votes are redistributed. The quota is recalculated based on the reduced number of active votes. {{reason}}
electRemainingExplanation: Only {{candidate}} remains to fill the last seat. They are elected without needing to reach the quota.
defeatRemainingExplanation: All seats are filled. {{candidate}} and any other remaining candidates are defeated.
breakTieExplanation: There is a tie between {{candidates}}. {{defeated}} is defeated by tie-break, and their votes are transferred.
countCompleteExplanation: All seats have been filled. The count is complete.
firstRoundSummary: First preferences are counted and the quota is calculated
finalRoundSummary: The final round — all seats are filled
subsequentRoundSummary: Surplus and defeated votes are redistributed
walkthrough.electDetail: {{candidate}} has received enough votes to exceed the Droop quota and is elected. Their surplus votes will transfer to remaining candidates based on voters' next preferences.
walkthrough.electRemainingDetail: Only {{candidate}} remains to fill the last seat, so they are elected without needing to reach the quota.
walkthrough.iterateDetail: Votes are redistributed to remaining candidates based on each ballot's next active preference.
walkthrough.defeatDetail: {{candidate}} has the fewest votes ({{reason}}) and is defeated. Their votes transfer to each ballot's next active preference.
walkthrough.defeatRemainingDetail: All seats are filled. {{candidate}} and any other remaining candidates are defeated.
walkthrough.breakTieDetail: There is a tie between [{{candidates}}]. {{defeated}} is defeated by tie-break and their votes are transferred.
Token Redeem meta description: Redeem your ballot token to access your voting page. This is your private link to cast your vote in the election.
This token has already been redeemed. Ea: This token has already been redeemed. Each token can only be used once. If you previously redeemed this token, you should have received a ballot link.
You have been invited to vote in this el: You have been invited to vote in this election. Click the button below to access your ballot.
Important: After continuing, you will re: Important: After continuing, you will receive a unique link to your ballot. This current link will no longer work. Do not share the ballot link with anyone, as it allows direct access to your vote.
quotaTooltip: Minimum number of votes needed to be elected (Droop quota)
votesTooltip: Active votes still in play (can be transferred)
residualTooltip: Exhausted votes that could not be transferred further
surplusTooltip: Votes exceeding the quota, transferred to next preferences
Define groups and seat allocation per gr: Define groups and seat allocation per group. Total group seats must equal total seats.
At least 2 groups are required for group: At least 2 groups are required for grouped elections
All groups must have a name: All groups must have a name
Sum of group seats must equal total seat: Sum of group seats must equal total seats
All candidates must be assigned to a gro: All candidates must be assigned to a group
Assign each candidate to a group. Candid: Assign each candidate to a group. Candidates in each group compete only for the seats allocated to that group.
Candidates are split into predefined gro: Candidates are split into predefined groups (e.g., female/male) with a specific number of seats allocated to each group. STV is run independently within each group. Use this to guarantee representation for each group — for example, in parity elections where a certain number of seats must be filled by each group.
When using this type, you define the gro: When using this type, you define the groups and their seat counts in the election setup, then assign each candidate to a group. A voter's ballot is evaluated separately per group: only candidates from that group are considered, preserving the voter's relative preference order.
The system shows elected candidates, opt: The system shows elected candidates, optional Copeland ranking, detailed counting rounds, and the pairwise comparison matrix. For grouped elections, results are displayed per group with separate counting logs for each group.
riskAssessment.pageDescription: Comprehensive risk assessment for the STVote electronic voting platform, including security threats, mitigations, and residual risks.
riskAssessment.intro: This document provides a comprehensive risk assessment for the STVote.eu platform. It evaluates potential security threats, their likelihood and impact, implemented mitigations, and residual risks.
riskAssessment.scopeTextPre: STVote.eu is designed for small to medium-scale elections where electronic voting is appropriate.
riskAssessment.scopeTextBold: Do not use this platform for high-stakes national elections
riskAssessment.scopeTextPost: where the risks require additional security measures such as physical voting, air-gapped systems, or formal security certifications.
riskAssessment.likelihoodValues: Very Low, Low, Medium, High, Very High
riskAssessment.impactValues: Very Low, Low, Medium, High, Critical
riskAssessment.residualRiskDesc: Risk remaining after mitigations are applied
An attacker intercepts a ballot token URL during distribution
Tokens are single-use UUIDs. Secure distribution channels (encrypted messaging, email) used by the electoral commission.
riskAssessment.threat.ballotTokenTheft.d: An attacker obtains a ballot token before the voter uses it
riskAssessment.threat.ballotTokenTheft.m: Tokens are single-use and can only be redeemed once. If a voter notices their token was used, they can report to electoral commission.
riskAssessment.threat.databaseCompromise: An attacker gains access to the database
Election results remain valid (ballots are public after election). Unredeemed tokens could be compromised. Filesystem access controls restrict database access to the application container only.
riskAssessment.threat.serverCompromise.d: An attacker gains control of the server
riskAssessment.threat.serverCompromise.m: Automatic security updates, minimal exposed ports, and firewalled configuration. Runs in Docker as non-root user. Caddy reverse proxy with automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt.
riskAssessment.threat.coercion.title: Coercion/Vote Buying
riskAssessment.threat.coercion.descripti: Voter is forced to vote a certain way or sells their vote
riskAssessment.threat.coercion.mitigatio: Ballot UUID is only known to the voter. However, voters can prove how they voted by sharing their ballot UUID. Unsuitable for elections with high coercion risk.
riskAssessment.threat.ddos.title: Denial of Service (DDoS)
riskAssessment.threat.ddos.description: Attackers overwhelm the server to prevent voting
riskAssessment.threat.ddos.mitigation: STVote.eu is protected by Cloudflare, providing enterprise-grade DDoS mitigation. Rate limiting at application level. Automatic TLS via Caddy. If service disruption occurs, voting period can be extended.
riskAssessment.threat.adminUuidLeakage.d: Election admin UUID is exposed allowing unauthorized modifications
riskAssessment.threat.adminUuidLeakage.m: Admin UUIDs should be kept confidential. Elections become locked after first vote is cast. Use secure URL sharing practices.
riskAssessment.threat.supplyChain.title: Supply Chain Attack
riskAssessment.threat.supplyChain.descri: Compromised dependency in software supply chain
riskAssessment.threat.supplyChain.mitiga: All source code is open source and auditable. Dependencies are scanned and regularly updated.
riskAssessment.threat.voterPrivacy.title: Voter Privacy Breach
riskAssessment.threat.voterPrivacy.descr: Linking ballots back to voters through timing or other metadata
riskAssessment.threat.voterPrivacy.mitig: Ballot-token association is permanently erased upon token redemption. No IP logging or tracking. Ballots are only revealed after election ends.
riskAssessment.threat.resultManipulation: Someone tries to manipulate the election results
All ballots are public after election ends. Anyone can verify the counting using the published algorithm. Results are auditable and reproducible.
riskAssessment.threat.phishing.title: Phishing Attacks
riskAssessment.threat.phishing.descripti: Attackers create fake voting sites to steal tokens or mislead voters
riskAssessment.threat.phishing.mitigatio: Electoral commission should educate voters about the official domain (stvote.eu). Uses HTTPS with valid certificates. Voters should verify the URL before entering tokens.
riskAssessment.threat.clientSideCompromi: Voter's device or browser is compromised (malware, malicious extensions)
Vote can only affect individual voter. No administrative access from client. Voters should use trusted devices and updated browsers. Consider using private/incognito mode.
riskAssessment.threat.lostTokenAccess.de: Voter loses their ballot token before voting
riskAssessment.threat.lostTokenAccess.mi: Electoral commission retains token list and can resend. Educate voters to store tokens securely. Tokens remain valid throughout voting period.
riskAssessment.threat.timingAnalysis.des: Observers correlate voting times with known voter activity
riskAssessment.threat.timingAnalysis.mit: Ballots only become public after election ends. No timestamps are exposed. Token redemption and ballot submission are separate events. Voters can redeem early and vote later.
riskAssessment.threat.hostingProvider.de: Cloud/hosting provider personnel access system data
riskAssessment.threat.hostingProvider.mi: Election results are publicly verifiable after closing. Database files reside on an encrypted Hetzner Block Storage volume (AES-256 at rest) with filesystem access controls.
riskAssessment.ballotAnonymizationText: The core security feature is the permanent erasure of the ballot token-to-ballot UUID association. When a voter redeems their token, a new ballot UUID is generated and the link between the token and ballot is never stored. This ensures voter anonymity while maintaining ballot integrity.
riskAssessment.noAuthText: The system uses knowledge-based access control (UUID tokens) rather than traditional authentication. This eliminates the need for user accounts, passwords, or personal data collection, reducing both privacy risks and implementation complexity.
riskAssessment.publicAuditabilityText: After an election ends, all ballots are made public. Anyone can verify the counting algorithm matches the published results. The complete source code for the counting algorithm, backend, frontend, and infrastructure is open source and auditable.
riskAssessment.singleUseTokensText: Each ballot token can only be redeemed once and only during the voting period. Tokens cannot be redeemed after the election closes, preventing late manipulation attempts.
riskAssessment.electionLockingText: Once the first vote is cast, an election becomes locked and its configuration (candidates, seats, times) cannot be modified. This prevents administrators from changing election parameters after voting has begun.
riskAssessment.technicalMeasures.deploym: Docker containers running as non-root user (UID 1000)
riskAssessment.technicalMeasures.tls.tex: Automatic HTTPS via Caddy reverse proxy with Let's Encrypt
riskAssessment.technicalMeasures.inputVa: All inputs validated for type safety and business logic
riskAssessment.technicalMeasures.atomici: Database transactions ensure consistency of critical operations
riskAssessment.technicalMeasures.openSou: All code publicly available for security review
riskAssessment.recommendationsIntro: To minimize risks, electoral commissions should:
riskAssessment.recommendation1.label: Distribute tokens securely:
riskAssessment.recommendation1.text: Use encrypted channels (Signal, secure email) to send ballot tokens to voters
riskAssessment.recommendation2.label: Keep admin UUIDs confidential:
riskAssessment.recommendation2.text: Only share admin URLs with trusted election administrators
riskAssessment.recommendation3.label: Assess coercion risk:
riskAssessment.recommendation3.text: Consider if voters might face pressure to vote certain ways. If high, use physical voting instead
riskAssessment.recommendation4.label: Communicate transparently:
riskAssessment.recommendation4.text: Inform voters about the security model and how their privacy is protected
riskAssessment.recommendation5.label: Plan for contingencies:
riskAssessment.recommendation5.text: Have backup plans if technical issues occur during voting period
riskAssessment.recommendation6.label: Self-hosting option:
riskAssessment.recommendation6.text: For maximum control and security, consider self-hosting using the open-source code
riskAssessment.openSourcePrefix: All source code is publicly available:
riskAssessment.conclusionPara1: STVote.eu provides a secure and transparent platform for running STV elections in contexts where electronic voting is appropriate. The system's security relies on cryptographic randomness (UUIDs), permanent anonymization of votes, public auditability, and open-source transparency.
riskAssessment.conclusionPara2Pre: The platform is suitable for community organizations, clubs, associations, and small-scale democratic processes. It is
riskAssessment.conclusionPara2Post: for high-stakes elections requiring additional security measures, formal certifications, or protection against nation-state level threats.
